summary: The smallest coherent path was to extend the existing sqlite host-binding/runtime contract instead of inventing new syntax: mutation runtime names now carry transaction metadata, and checker guards stop primitive leakage at the mutation boundary.
ergonomics: The existing host-binding manifest already carried enough schema codecs to add semantic mutation surfaces without touching the parser or emitter shape much.
Runtime scenario tests in `test/Main.hs` made this slice practical, but the file is large enough that finding the right insertion points is slower than it should be.
`scripts/verify-all.sh` working through `nix develop` avoided local toolchain drift; direct `cabal test` outside nix failed because `ghc` was unavailable.
follow_ups: Add a small helper/parser for sqlite runtime names so operation/isolation rules are centralized and easier to extend for future DB tasks.
Consider a first-class `SqliteTransaction` language/runtime surface if later tasks need transaction values to cross host boundaries explicitly.
Split the large runtime regression section in `test/Main.hs` into focused helpers/modules to reduce edit friction for future storage/runtime tasks.
warnings: `sqlite:mutate*` boundary checks intentionally skip the first two parameters (`SqliteConnection`, SQL text); later params and returns must stay semantic types.
Avoid record fields named `value` in quick runtime fixtures unless you confirm the emitted codec path, because one generated module path currently collides on that identifier in JS.
The full verifier emits a lot of unrelated dirty-tree warnings from `nix`; they are noisy but were not failures in this run.
tests_run: nix develop -c bash -lc 'cabal test clasp-compiler-test --test-options="-p \"sqlite runtime executes typed mutation bindings inside typed transaction boundaries with isolation and rollback semantics\""'
residual_risks: Nested sqlite transactions are implemented with savepoints; nested descriptors expose requested isolation metadata, but SQLite cannot strengthen the outer transaction mode after the first `BEGIN`.

The new mutation surface is runtime-name based (`sqlite:mutateOne[:isolation]`, `sqlite:mutateAll[:isolation]`) rather than a new compiler-known transaction type, so future slices may still want a first-class language surface.
